rules.message: Hardcoded secret-shaped literal (an API-key/password/token assignment, or a known cloud-key prefix) — a credential committed to source is exposed to every reader/fork/clone and cannot be rotated without a code change. Move it to an environment variable or a secrets manager (e.g. `process.env.API_KEY`) — but that only stops the NEXT leak: this value is already exposed via git history and any forks/clones that pulled it, so rotate/revoke the actual credential too. Suppress a vetted case with `// secret-ok`.

`req.body` (or a spread of it) passed directly into a database write (`create`/`update`/`upsert`/`updateMany`) — an attacker can set any field the model accepts (role, isAdmin, price, ownerId, ...) that the handler never intended to expose. Whitelist the fields you accept, or use a validated DTO. Suppress a vetted case with `// mass-assignment-ok`.

`$queryRawUnsafe`/`$executeRawUnsafe` called — these run the given SQL as-is with no parameterization, so any request-derived string interpolated into it is a SQL injection. Use `$queryRaw`/`$executeRaw` tagged templates (or pass values as separate arguments) so Prisma parameterizes the query. Suppress a vetted case with `// raw-sql-ok`.

A cookie is set (`res.cookie`/`setCookie`/`cookies.set`) without `httpOnly` in the same function — a session/auth cookie without it is readable by any script on the page, so an XSS bug becomes full session theft. Add `{ httpOnly: true }` (plus `secure`/`sameSite` as appropriate). Suppress a vetted case with `// cookie-ok`.

CORS origin set to `*` (a wildcard `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header, or `origin: '*'`) — lets any website make cross-origin requests to this endpoint, defeating same-origin protection. Return a specific allow-listed origin instead, especially on endpoints that accept cookies or auth headers. A wildcard is legitimate for a purely public, credential-less API (no cookies/auth headers involved) — suppress that case with `// cors-ok` rather than "fixing" it.

Password hashed/compared with a broken algorithm (MD5/SHA-1) or bcrypt at a single-digit cost factor — both are too cheap to resist offline brute force at billions of guesses/sec on commodity GPUs. Use bcrypt with a cost factor of at least 10 (bcrypt-specific — cost factor isn't a scrypt/argon2 unit), or argon2id/scrypt configured at OWASP-recommended memory/parallelism parameters. Suppress a vetted case with `// weak-hash-ok`.

A secret-shaped query parameter (`api_key`/`access_token`/`token`/`secret`) appears in a URL — URLs are logged by proxies, load balancers, browser history and the `Referer` header. Move the credential into an `Authorization` header (or body/cookie) instead. Suppress a vetted case with `// url-key-ok`.

JPA `@Query` built via string concatenation (`@Query("..." + ...)`) — if the concatenated part is request-derived, it's spliced straight into SQL/JPQL, the same SQL injection risk as building any query string by hand; a constant/literal fragment isn't injectable but still obscures the risk and makes future refactors unsafe. Use named parameters (`:param` with `@Param`) or a parameterized query instead. Suppress a vetted case with `// query-concat-ok`.

`redirect(...)` is called in a function that also reads `req.query`/`req.params`/`req.body` — an unchecked request-derived target lets an attacker send victims to an arbitrary external site (open redirect), a common phishing/OAuth-token-theft vector. Validate the target against an allow-list of known internal paths. Suppress a vetted case with `// redirect-ok`.

An outbound HTTP call (`fetch`/`axios`/`got`) is made in a function that also reads `req.query`/`req.params`/`req.body` — if that value becomes part of the URL, the server can be made to request attacker-chosen hosts (SSRF), reaching internal services or a metadata endpoint. Validate the target against an allow-list of hosts. Suppress a vetted case with `// ssrf-ok`.

A filesystem call (`fs`/`fsp.*`, `readFile`/`writeFile`) reads a `path.join(...)` path derived from request input — an unvalidated `..` segment lets an attacker escape the intended directory (path traversal). Reject `..` segments, or verify the resolved path stays under the base directory. Suppress a vetted case with `// traversal-ok`.

`credentials: true` appears in a file that also configures `origin: '*'` — browsers actually REJECT (refuse to expose the response for) a wildcard `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` combined with credentials, so this exact configuration is broken/non-functional rather than exploitable as written. The real risk: to "fix" the breakage, code often reflects the request's `Origin` header back with `credentials: true` still set — that treats any origin as trusted and IS the actual vulnerability. Return a specific allow-listed origin (never `*`, never an echoed `Origin`) whenever `credentials: true` is set. Suppress a vetted case with `// cors-cred-ok`.

`jwt.sign(...)` is called where `expiresIn` never appears in the same function — a token with no expiry is valid forever, so a leak (logs, XSS, a compromised client) can be replayed indefinitely. Pass `{ expiresIn: '...' }` (or set `exp` in the payload) so tokens expire. Suppress a vetted case with `// jwt-expiry-ok`.

`Math.random()` is used on the same line as a token/otp/nonce/session-id/secret-shaped identifier — it's a predictable PRNG, not cryptographically secure, so a value generated this way can be guessed or brute-forced. Use `crypto.randomBytes(n).toString('hex')` or `crypto.randomUUID()`. Suppress a vetted case with `// weak-random-ok`.

A secret/token/signature/hmac/api-key-shaped identifier is compared with `===`/`!==` — equality operators short-circuit on the first differing byte, so the comparison time itself can leak a timing signal about the value to an attacker positioned to measure it. Use `crypto.timingSafeEqual(...)` on fixed-length buffers instead. Suppress a vetted case with `// timing-ok`.

A raw error object is sent directly to the client (`res.status(5xx).send/json(err)`, or Hono's `c.json(err)`) — errors often carry stack traces, file paths, or internal config that help an attacker map the system. Log server-side and send a generic message (e.g. `{ error: 'Internal server error' }`) instead. Suppress a vetted case with `// error-leak-ok`.

A server-only-shaped env var (`SECRET`/`PRIVATE`/`SERVICE_ROLE`/`SERVICE_KEY`) is referenced from frontend code — build tools inline `process.env.*`/`import.meta.env.*` into the shipped JS bundle, making it readable in devtools. If the value is genuinely secret, the only correct fix is moving its use behind a backend endpoint (proxy it server-side) — do not just rename it with a `NEXT_PUBLIC_`/`VITE_` prefix. Only reach for that prefix if the value was never actually secret to begin with (e.g. a public config flag flagged by mistake). Suppress a vetted case with `// fe-env-ok`.

A token/JWT-shaped value is written to `localStorage` — readable by any script on the page, so a single XSS bug anywhere on the origin can exfiltrate it with no further exploitation. Default fix: store it in an `httpOnly` cookie with `sameSite` set, plus CSRF-token protection. This isn't a full fix either — cookies reintroduce CSRF risk (mitigated by `sameSite`/CSRF tokens), and an active XSS bug can still ride an existing cookie session in-place — but it does stop direct token theft/exfiltration. Accepted alternative: an in-memory access token with a rotating refresh token, or a Backend-For-Frontend (BFF) pattern. Suppress a vetted case with `// ls-token-ok`.

A password-shaped literal is hardcoded — a direct assignment (`password = "..."`) or a JDBC `getConnection(url, user, password)` call with literal credentials — exposed to every reader of the repo and impossible to rotate without a code change. Read it from an environment variable, an excluded properties file, or a secrets manager — but that only stops the NEXT leak: this password is already exposed via git history and any forks/clones that pulled it, so rotate/revoke the actual credential too. Suppress a vetted case with `// java-pwd-ok`.

`DocumentBuilderFactory`/`SAXParserFactory.newInstance()` with no recognized XXE guard in the same method — a default-configured XML parser resolves external entities, so crafted XML can read local files, perform SSRF, or exhaust memory. Set `disallow-doctype-decl` to `true`; `FEATURE_SECURE_PROCESSING` alone does NOT disable external entity resolution and is not sufficient on its own. If DOCTYPEs are genuinely required, set both `external-general-entities` and `external-parameter-entities` to `false` instead. Each recognized guard pattern silences this finding independently (any one alone is enough to veto it). Suppress a vetted case with `// xxe-ok`.

`ObjectInputStream.readObject()` is called in this function — Java's native deserialization invokes arbitrary code paths (gadget chains), so deserializing an attacker-controlled byte stream can lead to remote code execution. Kept at `warning` because this local analysis can't prove the deserialized payload is attacker-reachable — treat it as CRITICAL wherever the input actually comes from a request body/query/header/upload. Use JSON/Protobuf instead, or validate the stream with an allow-list `ObjectInputFilter`. Suppress a vetted case with `// deser-ok`.

`new File(...)` is constructed in a function that also reads `request.getParameter(...)` — an unvalidated parameter flowing into the path lets an attacker escape the intended directory and read/write files outside it. Reject `..` segments, or resolve and compare against the base directory before use. Suppress a vetted case with `// java-traversal-ok`.

`new Random()` is used on the same line as a token/session/otp/nonce-shaped identifier — `java.util.Random` is a predictable PRNG, not cryptographically secure, so a value generated this way can be predicted or brute-forced. Use `java.security.SecureRandom` instead. Suppress a vetted case with `// java-random-ok`.

An exception's stack trace/message (`printStackTrace()`/`.getMessage()`) touches the HTTP response (`getWriter()`, `ResponseEntity`, `@ResponseBody`) in the same method — if it reaches the client, it exposes internal class names and file paths. Log server-side and return a generic error body instead. Suppress a vetted case with `// stacktrace-ok`.

TLS certificate/hostname verification is disabled (a trust-all `X509TrustManager`, `ALLOW_ALL_HOSTNAME_VERIFIER`, or a `setHostnameVerifier` lambda always returning `true`) — accepts any certificate for any host, so the app can't detect a man-in-the-middle. Use the default (or a properly validating custom) trust manager/verifier. Suppress a vetted case with `// trust-all-ok`.

A connection-string URL embeds a password directly in source (`scheme://user:pass@host`) — anyone with repo read access effectively owns the datastore, and git history preserves the credential even after it's later removed from HEAD. Move the URL to environment/secret configuration AND rotate the credential (treat it as already compromised). Loopback hosts (localhost/127.0.0.1) and placeholder-shaped passwords (`changeme`, `mypwd`, ...) are not flagged; suppress a remaining vetted sample with `// conn-cred-ok`.

A PEM-format private key (`-----BEGIN ... PRIVATE KEY-----`) is committed to source — the PEM header is unambiguous, so unlike a heuristic secret-shaped literal there's no plausible non-key reading of this text. The moment this repo is shared (a fork, a clone, a public push) the key is compromised, and git history keeps it even after the file is later removed. Rotate the key immediately and move it to a secret store (a vault, KMS, or CI secret) instead — an actual PEM header sitting in a test fixture is still a committed key, so this rule scans test paths too, not just application code. Suppress a vetted case (e.g. a throwaway key generated only for a test, never used against a real service) with `// private-key-ok`.

A live-mode vendor credential appears in its recognized production-token format — Stripe `sk_live_`/`rk_live_`, a GitHub `ghp_`/`gho_` personal access token, a Slack `xox[bpars]-` token, or a Google API key (`AIza...`). Format alone identifies these as production secrets, and a committed credential is a leaked credential the instant the repo is shared, regardless of later git-history cleanup. Deliberately NOT matched: Stripe `sk_test_...` — a test-mode key is not a production credential, so flagging it would be noise, not signal. Rotate the credential through the vendor's own key-rotation/revocation flow immediately, then move the replacement into env/secret storage. Suppress a vetted case with `// vendor-token-ok`.

`algorithm(s): 'none'` appears in a JWT-related file — `alg: none` turns signature verification off entirely, so a caller can mint an arbitrary token that validates with any payload it wants. There is no legitimate production use of this algorithm. Remove `none` from the allowed/signing algorithm list and use a real algorithm (`HS256`/`RS256`/...), pinning `algorithms` explicitly on the verifying side too so a future token can't downgrade itself. Suppress a vetted case (e.g. a local test harness that intentionally exercises the none-alg attack) with `// jwt-none-ok`.

A dynamic segment (a template-literal `${...}` or a string-concatenation `+`) is spliced directly into `exec`/`execSync`'s shell-command-string argument — the shell interprets that whole string, so any dynamic part reaching it is command injection, whether or not this rule can prove it's request-derived (see boundary note below). Use `execFile`/`spawn` with an argv array instead — arguments passed as separate array elements are never shell-interpreted, which is exactly why this rule does not fire on `execFile`/`spawn` calls. Matched shapes are child_process-scoped: a bare/destructured `exec(...)`/`execSync(...)` call, or a `child_process.`/`childProcess.`/`cp.` member call, in a file that mentions `child_process` at all — a RegExp's own `pattern.exec(...)` (or any other dotted receiver) does not fire. Boundary: `security/taint-flow` also flags `exec`/`execSync` as a sink, but only when a request-derived source co-occurs in the same `.ts`/`.tsx` function; this rule needs no source, fires on the interpolation shape alone, and also covers plain `.js` files — treat the two as complementary, not redundant. Suppress a vetted case with `// shell-exec-ok`.

`jwt.sign(payload, "...")` is called with a positional string-literal signing secret — anyone who reads this line can forge a validly-signed token for any payload they want. This is a distinct shape from `hardcoded-secret`: that rule's `assignment` pattern needs a `key: value`/`key = value` shape, so a positional literal passed straight as `jwt.sign`'s second argument is uncovered by it — this rule closes that gap. Move the signing secret into an environment variable or secrets manager, and rotate the exposed key (it's already leaked via git history and any forks/clones that pulled it). Suppress a vetted case with `// jwt-secret-ok`.

`ignoreExpiration: true` or `verify: false` appears in a file that imports a JWT library (`jsonwebtoken`/`jose`/a `jwt` specifier) — either option turns off a check `jwt.verify` exists to perform: `ignoreExpiration: true` accepts an expired token, `verify: false` accepts one with no signature check at all (an outright auth bypass). The require-file gate on a JWT-library import is load-bearing here: bare `verify: false` also appears in plenty of unrelated configs (bundlers, linters, ...), and without the gate this rule would fire on those too. Remove the option (or set it to the safe default) so `jwt.verify` performs its normal checks. Suppress a vetted case with `// jwt-verify-ok`.

`sendFile`/`download` is called with a `req.params`/`req.query`/`req.body`-derived path — the sibling `path-traversal` rule catches `fs`/`path.join` reads built from request input in the same shape, but does not cover Express's file-serving APIs (`res.sendFile`/`res.download`) directly, so this rule fills that gap. An unvalidated `..` segment lets an attacker read arbitrary files off disk via path traversal. Reject `..` segments, or resolve the path and confirm it stays under the intended base directory, before passing it in. v1 is kept honest rather than clever: a same-line `path.basename(...)` call vetoes the finding by simple textual co-presence, not by proving it actually wraps the request-derived argument — a real sanitizer elsewhere in the function is not detected. Suppress a vetted case with `// sendfile-ok`.

`credentials: true` co-occurs on one line with `origin: true` (cors()'s reflect-any-origin mode) or `origin: req.headers...` (manually echoing the request's Origin header) — either shape treats any origin as trusted while allowing cookies/credentials, which is the actual exploitable CORS misconfiguration (unlike a literal `'*'`, which browsers reject alongside credentials). Sibling boundary: `cors-wildcard`/`cors-credentials-wildcard` cover the literal `'*'` shape; this rule covers the REFLECTION shape those two miss. Return a specific allow-listed origin instead of reflecting the request's origin. Known limitation, not desired behavior: this is a single-line co-occurrence check, so a multi-line CORS options object (each key on its own line) is missed — see the pinned fixture documenting this gap. Suppress a vetted case with `// cors-reflect-ok`.

This line uses a template-engine syntax that emits UNescaped HTML: EJS `<%- %>`, Handlebars `{{{ }}}` triple-stache, or Mustache `{{& }}` — if any interpolated value is user-influenced, this is reflected/stored server-side XSS. Use the escaped output form instead (EJS `<%= %>`, Handlebars/Mustache `{{ }}`), or route the value through a vetted sanitizer when intentional raw HTML is actually required. Sibling boundary: `browser/unsafe-html-sink` catches the client-side DOM-sink half of this class (`innerHTML` assignment in browser code); this rule catches the server-side template-engine half that produces the HTML in the first place — complementary, not overlapping. Scoped to template file extensions only (`.ejs`/`.hbs`/`.handlebars`/`.mustache`/`.njk`/`.nunjucks`), since these tokens only mean "unescaped output" inside a template file. (Pug's buffered `!= expr` form is deliberately NOT covered: it is lexically indistinguishable from Pug/Nunjucks's loose-inequality operator `!=`, so flagging it would false-fire on every `a != b` conditional — a never-guess omission, not an oversight.) Suppress a vetted case with `// template-unescaped-ok` — but note `//` is not a comment in EJS/Handlebars/Mustache/Nunjucks, so an inline marker there renders as literal page output; prefer disabling the rule via config (`rules: { "be-security/template-unescaped-output": "off" }`) for template files where inline suppression isn't clean.

A `res.send`/`res.write`/`res.end` call co-occurs with request input (`req.query`/`req.params`/`req.body`/`req.headers`) AND an HTML-shaped marker (a literal opening tag like `<div>`/`<html>`, or a `Content-Type: text/html` header) in the same function, with no sanitizer call anywhere in it — this is a co-occurrence heuristic, not a proof that the request value actually reaches the HTML output (same honesty as `be-reliability/multi-write-no-tx`'s documented approximation): it flags the shape, not a traced data flow. Escape/sanitize request-derived values before splicing them into HTML, set an explicit `application/json` content type for pure API responses, or use a template engine with auto-escaping instead of building markup by hand. Sibling boundary: `security/taint-flow` also does source+sink co-occurrence for `.ts`/`.tsx`, but its sink list does not include `res.send`/`res.write`/`res.end` — this rule covers exactly the class taint-flow misses, and also runs on plain `.js`/`.cjs`/`.mjs` files taint-flow doesn't scan. Suppress a vetted case with `// html-response-ok`.

A string literal that opens or closes an HTML tag (`"
"`, `""`, ...) is concatenated with a non-literal (`+ someVar` / `someVar +`) in a file that also mentions a `res`/`response`/content-type-`text/html` server-response context — building HTML by concatenating a variable into tag markup is an injection sink if that variable is user-influenced. Use a template engine with auto-escaping, or a sanitizer, instead of hand-assembling markup. Kept `warning` and deployed-surface-excluded because this is shape-only and FP-prone (logging strings, test fixtures, non-response string building all read the same way to a regex) — the `require_file` gate on a response/content-type mention is there specifically to bias this toward real server-response context rather than firing on every HTML-shaped concatenation in the repo. Sibling boundary: `html-response-from-request` needs the request-input+HTML-marker co-occurrence inside one function; this rule needs neither — it fires on the concatenation shape alone, so treat the two as complementary. Suppress a vetted case with `// html-concat-ok`.

Content-Security-Policy is explicitly turned off or weakened: Helmet's `contentSecurityPolicy: false`, a `Content-Security-Policy` header value containing `unsafe-inline`, or a `default-src` directive containing a wildcard `*` — CSP is the browser's last line of defense against XSS once a payload has already been injected, so disabling/weakening it removes a mitigation that has nothing to do with whether the injection itself is possible. Keep a restrictive policy: enable Helmet's default CSP (or a hand-configured one) instead of `false`, drop `unsafe-inline` in favor of nonces/hashes, and scope `default-src`/`script-src` to specific allow-listed origins instead of `*`. Kept at `warning` because this is a mitigation-ABSENCE rule (defense-in-depth), not proof of an exploitable injection by itself. Suppress a vetted case with `// csp-disabled-ok`.
